guidelines.guideline_description: A Class is a classifier that may own zero or more Property elements as attributes and zero or more Operation elements as structural features.
An Interface is a classifier whose features consist exclusively of Operation elements and which must be realized by client classifiers implementing all its Operations.
An Enumeration is a classifier defining a finite set of EnumerationLiteral elements and does not own additional Properties or Operations beyond its literals.
A DataType is a value‐semantic classifier that may own Property elements but does not define identity semantics and is distinct from PrimitiveType.
A PrimitiveType is a predefined classifier with no owned Property or Operation elements and represents an atomic value.
A Package defines a namespace and may own any number of ModelElement instances; deletion of the Package removes its owned contents recursively.
A Property must be owned either by a Classifier (as an attribute) or by an Association (as an association end) and must reference a Type with defined multiplicity bounds.
A Property owned by a Classifier represents an attribute with a name, Type reference, multiplicity bounds, and optional default or derived designation.
A Property owned by an Association represents an association end with a role name, multiplicity, and navigability setting.
An Operation is a behavioral feature of a Classifier with zero or more Parameter elements and an optional return Type.
An Association connects two or more Classifier elements and defines Links across its association ends represented by Property elements.
An association end Property may set its navigability flag to true, indicating a directed navigation from the opposite end.
An Association end Property may have AggregationKind = shared, indicating a weak whole–part relationship where parts may outlive the whole.
An Association end Property with AggregationKind = composite enforces that each part is contained in at most one composite and is deleted with its composite.
A Generalization links a specific Classifier to a more general Classifier; the inheritance graph must be acyclic to avoid circular specialization.
A Realization is an abstraction relationship where a client Classifier implements or realizes the specification of a supplier Interface or Classifier.
A Dependency is a relationship from a client ModelElement to a supplier ModelElement, indicating that changes in the supplier may impact the client.
An AssociationClass is a construct that simultaneously behaves as an Association and as a Classifier owning its own Property and Operation elements.
A QualifiedAssociation uses one or more qualifier Property elements at an association end to partition and index the set of associated instances.
A Constraint attaches to one or more ModelElement instances and specifies a side‐effect free Boolean condition that must hold in valid models.
An EnumerationLiteral is a named instance owned by an Enumeration classifier and denotes one of the discrete allowed values for that Enumeration.
A Parameter is owned by an Operation, has a direction (in, out, return) and a Type reference, and defines the signature of the Operation.

agent1_capabilities: How do you differentiate between aggregation and composition in UML Class Diagrams?
What structural rule defines an AssociationClass in a class diagram?
How are multiplicities and navigability represented on association ends as Property elements?
What constraints must a Generalization hierarchy satisfy to avoid cycles?
When does a Realization relationship apply between a Class and an Interface?
How does a QualifiedAssociation partition associated instances?
What defines a valid Constraint on a Class in a class diagram?
Which classifiers can be contained within a Package?
What distinguishes a DataType from a PrimitiveType in UML class diagrams?
How are Operation parameters and return types defined structurally?
